mnemonics.title: BMTC Attacks
mnemonics.content: Four main public key attack categories: Brute-force (try all keys), Mathematical (factorization, discrete log), Timing (side-channel), Chosen-ciphertext (manipulate ciphertexts). Think: 'Bad Mathematicians Time Cryptanalysis.' Remember BMTC!

mnemonics.title: Factorization Breaks RSA
mnemonics.content: RSA security = difficulty of factoring n = p×q. If attacker factors n, they get p and q → compute φ(n) = (p-1)(q-1) → compute d from e → game over. Methods: Trial division (slow), Quadratic Sieve, GNFS (best). 829 bits factored so far. Use 2048+ bits!

mnemonics.title: Timing Attack = Statistical Key Recovery
mnemonics.content: Timing attacks measure how long operations take. If bit=1: multiply (slower), if bit=0: square (faster). Measure thousands of operations, statistics reveal private key bits one by one. Defense: Constant-Time code (same speed regardless of key values) or Blinding (randomize inputs).

mnemonics.title: CCA = Malleable Ciphertext Attack
mnemonics.content: Chosen Ciphertext Attack: Attacker modifies ciphertext C to C' = C×r^e, gets system to decrypt C', obtains M'=M×r, divides by r to get M. This works on RAW RSA! Defense: Use padding schemes like OAEP (makes ciphertext non-malleable). Never use raw RSA!

mnemonics.title: Quantum Threat = Shor's Algorithm
mnemonics.content: Shor's Algorithm (1994) on quantum computers can factor integers in POLYNOMIAL time (vs exponential classical). Would break RSA, DSA, DH, ECC in hours. Not yet practical (need large quantum computers), but coming. Solution: Post-Quantum Cryptography (lattice-based, code-based, hash-based).

keyPoints: Public key cryptanalysis exploits mathematical weaknesses, implementation flaws, or protocol vulnerabilities.
Brute force attacks are infeasible for 2048+ bit RSA keys (would take millions of years).
Mathematical attacks like GNFS can factor RSA moduli; 829-bit keys have been broken, making 2048+ bits necessary.
Timing attacks exploit implementation details to statistically recover private keys; constant-time code is essential.
Chosen ciphertext attacks (CCA) can break raw RSA by manipulating ciphertexts; OAEP padding provides defense.
Man-in-the-middle attacks intercept key exchange; PKI with certificate validation prevents this.
Quantum computing (Shor's algorithm) poses a future threat to all current public key systems.
Defense strategy: use large keys (2048+ bits), proper padding (OAEP/PSS), constant-time implementations, and PKI.
commonMistakes: Believing that larger keys always make attacks proportionally harder; some attacks have sub-exponential complexity.
Thinking mathematical attacks require breaking laws of physics; they're just very computationally intensive.
Assuming timing attacks are theoretical; they're practical and have been demonstrated on real systems.
Believing padding is optional or just for formatting; it's essential for security against CCA.
Thinking MITM attacks only apply to unencrypted channels; they can intercept public key exchange.
Assuming quantum computers will never be built; preparation for post-quantum cryptography should start now.
Confusing attack complexity (theoretical) with attack feasibility (practical with current resources).
